votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately computing the bounds, generating the sequence, filtering for even numbers, and comparing the result.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, computing the same bounds, generating the sequence of integers, filtering for even numbers, and comparing the result to 'res'.
The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, computing the same bounds, generating the inclusive range, filtering for even numbers, and checking equality with the result.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it computes lo as min(a,b), hi as min(max(a,b), 9), generates the range from lo to hi inclusive when lo <= hi, filters for even numbers (i % 2 == 0 matches Nat.even), and returns an empty list otherwise. The comparison res == expected matches the Coq equality check.

Python mirrors Coq: computes lo and hi the same way, builds the inclusive range lo..hi, filters even numbers, and returns [] when lo > hi.
Matches Coq: computes lo=min(a,b), hi=min(max(a,b),9), if lo<=hi returns evens in [lo..hi], else []. Compares res to this list.
Both compute lo=min(a,b), hi=min(max(a,b),9); if lo<=hi they produce the even numbers from lo to hi inclusive, else []. The Python returns whether res equals this list, matching the Coq spec.
